reasoning: The series TLAACBW027SBOG (Total Assets of US Commercial Banks) exhibits a strong long-term upward structural trend (growing roughly $740 billion per year, or about +$14 billion per week on average). In the very short term, the series is highly noisy and subject to weekly fluctuations. As of the March 12 data point (23,877), the series experienced a minor drop from the previous week (23,890) and is currently slightly above its longer-term linear trendline. Because of this short-term noise and potential mean-reversion, the probability of an increase over just one week (by March 23) is roughly a coin toss or slightly below (the statistical tool estimated 0.36; I predict 0.42 to account for underlying drift). Over longer horizons, the upward drift strongly dominates the weekly volatility. Standard macroeconomic forecasting of nominal, non-stationary aggregates means that uncertainty about the *direction* of change actually decreases as the time horizon grows. Thus, despite generic forecasting guidelines that uncertainty increases over time, the probability of exceeding a static starting threshold approaches 1.0 for drifting series. Consequently, the probabilities are monotonically increasing as the positive drift outweighs the short-term variance.
